title: CSV to parquet
text:
Yes — essentially always. The argument is boring and overwhelming:

Schema. CSV has no types. Every read has to either guess or be handed a schema. Parquet stores the schema with the file.
Columnar I/O. PLIDA-style tables have dozens to hundreds of columns but most queries touch a handful. Parquet lets the reader fetch only those columns; CSV forces a full row scan.
Compression. Parquet compression is strong, and explicit zstd is worth asking for. On this corpus the 44.5 GB MBS 2015 file shrank to 18.7 GB even before the layout pass below; on the focused itr_context rewrite, explicit zstd cut the footprint from 1.82 GB to 1.22 GB.
Row groups and zonemaps. Parquet stores per-row-group statistics. If you filter on INCM_YR = 2014, DuckDB/arrow can skip entire row groups whose min/max for INCM_YR does not overlap 2014.
Interop. Every engine we care about — arrow, duckdb, polars, pandas, pyarrow — reads parquet natively. CSV has a thousand dialects.

The only reason not to convert is if the file is too small to bother, and in that case the conversion is free anyway.

title: CSV to parquet
section: How we convert without running out of memory
text:
How we convert without running out of memory
A naive readr::read_csv() on a 45-50 GB source file is out of the question on 32 GB of RAM. The current project default is DuckDB’s streaming CSV reader feeding its parquet writer directly:
library(duckdb)
library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb())
dbExecute(con, "SET threads TO 8")
dbExecute(con, "SET memory_limit = '20GB'")
dbExecute(con, "SET preserve_insertion_order = false")

dbExecute(con, "
  COPY (
    SELECT * FROM read_csv_auto('dhda-mbs/madipge-mbs-d-claims-2015.csv')
  ) TO '/offline/using-plida-data/parquet/dhda-mbs'
  (FORMAT PARQUET, COMPRESSION zstd, ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000, ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20)
")
Five details in that snippet are worth spelling out:

read_csv_auto() is still a streaming bulk reader. DuckDB is not materialising the whole CSV in R memory first.
COMPRESSION zstd is worth setting explicitly here too.
ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000 lands in the same middle ground as the Arrow layout benchmark below: large enough to compress well, small enough to avoid a metadata explosion.
ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20 gives about five million rows per output file, which is the same basic target as the old Arrow pipeline’s max_rows_per_file = 5e6.
preserve_insertion_order = false is a DuckDB performance setting worth flipping for bulk import/export work where output row order is irrelevant.

If you only need a subset of columns, DuckDB can do that too: put the SELECT col_a, col_b, ... inside the COPY (...) TO ... query and the projection stays inside the engine.

title: CSV to parquet
section: Layout details are worth caring about
text:
Layout details are worth caring about
The Arrow and DuckDB docs both make the same point: Parquet is not one format, it is a family of layouts. File size, compression, partitioning, and row-group size all matter. So I rewrote the 1.8 GB itr_context parquet in a few variants and reran one year-filter query plus one full groupby in both engines.

Three takeaways matter for this project:

Explicit zstd is worth it. On itr_context, the current layout was 1.82 GB; the explicit zstd + 250k rewrite was 1.22 GB.
Year partitioning helps year filters when the table is actually multi-year. On the same table, DuckDB’s incm_yr == 2015 count fell from about 0.080s on the current layout to 0.028s on the year-partitioned layout; Arrow moved from 0.076s to 0.066s.
Tiny row groups are bad. The 10k-row-group variant made the full groupby much slower: Arrow went from about 0.149s to 0.515s, DuckDB from 0.100s to 0.235s.

The numbers are small because itr_context is only 1.8 GB, but the direction is the point. This is exactly what the docs predict. The practical rule: use zstd, keep row groups medium-sized, and only partition on columns you actually filter on a lot. For PLIDA that usually means year on multi-year panel-style tables; it does not mean inventing partitions for a single-year fact table like MBS 2015.

title: CSV to parquet
section: What about the CSV-to-DuckDB direct path?
text:
What about the CSV-to-DuckDB direct path?
DuckDB can ingest CSV directly with read_csv_auto or COPY ... FROM 'x.csv', so in principle you could skip the parquet intermediate and go straight from CSV to DuckDB-native storage. I do not recommend it for this kind of project, for three reasons:

Parquet is a useful artefact on its own — other tools (arrow, polars, pyarrow) can read it without a DuckDB connection.
Ingesting CSV-to-DuckDB-table takes the same time as CSV-to-parquet plus parquet-to-DuckDB-table, and you end up with only the DuckDB copy.
If your DuckDB file ever corrupts or you want to reshard the database, you have to re-read the CSVs. With parquet in place, the DuckDB rebuild is cheap.

The one exception is when you know you will only ever use DuckDB and the CSVs are small enough that a single-shot ingest is fine — then CREATE TABLE AS SELECT * FROM read_csv_auto(...) is slightly more convenient.






NoteShould we just use DuckDB for everything now?



No. This benchmark says DuckDB is the better CSV → parquet converter on this machine. It does not mean Arrow stops being useful.
Arrow still matters in this project for two reasons:

it is a very convenient reader/query layer over parquet when spinning up a DuckDB database is overkill;
parquet remains an ecosystem format, and Arrow is the most natural way to stay inside that ecosystem from R.

So the revised rule is narrower and more defensible: use DuckDB to build the parquet, keep parquet as the canonical intermediate, and use DuckDB or Arrow downstream depending on the query.
Code

Key findings baked into the site

CSV → parquet: 44.5 GB in 122s, peak RSS 1.76 GB (arrow streaming)

Case study pages use a conversational, direct tone modelled on R at Grattan. Reference/benchmark pages are more formal. See memory file feedback_tone.md for details.

Things to watch out for

_system/base-spine.csv in fplida can disappear during regeneration. The project uses abs-core/plidage-core-demog as the demographic spine instead.
ato-pit_itr/ contains 4 distinct schemas (context, ded-exp-off, inc-loss, whld-debt) that must be split into separate parquet subdirs before DuckDB ingest.
DuckDB memory_limit should always be set explicitly (~20 GB on a 32 GB machine). Default will claim all RAM and collide with R.
Synthetic data (fplida) does not model job switching — all STP switch counts are 0. The pipeline and timings are real; the results are not.
CS04 creates and deletes a temporary cs04_working.duckdb during render. Don’t interrupt the render mid-flight.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
text:
Window functions are the bread and butter of panel data work. “Did this person change employer?” is LAG(). “What’s their rolling average income?” is AVG() OVER (ROWS ...). “When did their health service use spike?” is a windowed comparison to a trailing mean.
On small data, you write the window function and move on. On PLIDA-scale data — 3.5 billion STP rows across 30 million people — you hit hard memory limits that force a different approach. This page documents what we tried, what failed, and what works.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
text:
The task
Detect employer switches in STP: a person whose ABN_HASH_TRUNC (employer identifier) changes from one week to the next.
We have 697 million rows for a single financial year (FY2122), or 3.5 billion across all 5 years. The machine has 32 GB RAM, with 24 GB allocated to DuckDB.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
section: What we tried and what happened
text:
What we tried and what happened

Attempt 1: LAG() on the full table
The textbook approach:
CREATE TABLE switches AS
WITH lagged AS (
  SELECT *,
    LAG(ABN_HASH_TRUNC) OVER (
      PARTITION BY SYNTHETIC_AEUID
      ORDER BY WEEK_ENDING
    ) AS prev_employer
  FROM stp
)
SELECT * FROM lagged
WHERE prev_employer IS NOT NULL
  AND ABN_HASH_TRUNC != prev_employer;
Result: out of memory. DuckDB tried to sort 3.5 billion rows, spilled 166 GB to disk, and ran out of disk space.


Attempt 2: LAG() on 1 year (700M rows)
Filtered to FY2122 only, materialised as a separate table first.
Result: out of memory. Even on a fresh DuckDB database with only 5.5 GB of data, the LAG() window function exceeded 24 GB of RAM trying to sort and materialise 700M rows across 30M partitions.


Attempt 3: Self-join on 1 year (700M rows)
Instead of LAG(), join the table to itself on (person, week = week + 7 days):
SELECT t1.SYNTHETIC_AEUID, t1.WEEK_ENDING,
       t1.ABN_HASH_TRUNC AS new_employer,
       t2.ABN_HASH_TRUNC AS prev_employer
FROM stp_1yr t1
INNER JOIN stp_1yr t2
  ON t1.SYNTHETIC_AEUID = t2.SYNTHETIC_AEUID
  AND t1.WEEK_ENDING = t2.WEEK_ENDING + INTERVAL '7 days'
WHERE t1.ABN_HASH_TRUNC != t2.ABN_HASH_TRUNC;
Result: out of memory. The hash table for 700M rows still exceeded 24 GB.






ImportantThe hard lesson



On a 32 GB machine, neither LAG() nor a self-join can process 700M rows in one shot. LAG fails because the sort is too large. The self-join fails because the hash table is too large. The scale of person-week PLIDA data breaks both approaches at the table level.




Attempt 4: Chunk by person hash bucket
Partition people into 10 buckets using hash(SYNTHETIC_AEUID) % 10. Process each bucket independently. Each bucket has ~70M rows — well within the 24 GB memory budget.
-- Add a bucket column at ingest
CREATE TABLE stp_1yr AS
SELECT *, hash(SYNTHETIC_AEUID) % 10 AS bucket
FROM read_parquet('stp/stp-fy2122*.parquet')
WHERE ABN_HASH_TRUNC IS NOT NULL;

-- Process one bucket at a time
-- (LAG version)
INSERT INTO switches
WITH lagged AS (
  SELECT *, LAG(ABN_HASH_TRUNC) OVER (
    PARTITION BY SYNTHETIC_AEUID ORDER BY WEEK_ENDING
  ) AS prev_employer
  FROM stp_1yr WHERE bucket = 0
)
SELECT ... FROM lagged
WHERE ABN_HASH_TRUNC != prev_employer;

-- (Self-join version)
INSERT INTO switches
SELECT t1.*, t2.ABN_HASH_TRUNC AS prev_employer
FROM stp_1yr t1
INNER JOIN stp_1yr t2
  ON t1.SYNTHETIC_AEUID = t2.SYNTHETIC_AEUID
  AND t1.WEEK_ENDING = t2.WEEK_ENDING + INTERVAL '7 days'
WHERE t1.bucket = 0 AND t2.bucket = 0
  AND t1.ABN_HASH_TRUNC != t2.ABN_HASH_TRUNC;
Result: success.




Per bucket
All 10 buckets




LAG
2.3 s
~23 s (projected)


Self-join
2.0 s
20.0 s (measured)



At 70M rows per bucket, both LAG() and the self-join complete in about 2 seconds. Processing all 10 buckets sequentially takes 20 seconds for the full 700M-row year. Extrapolating to all 5 years: about 100 seconds for the entire 3.5 billion row STP corpus.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
text:
How the self-join works
The self-join is worth understanding even though it’s not faster than LAG() at the same scale — because it works differently, and in some contexts that matters.
LAG() says “for each row, look back one position within this person’s timeline.” To define “one position back”, DuckDB must sort all rows by (person, week). That’s O(N log N).
The self-join says “for each row, find the row for the same person exactly one week earlier.” DuckDB builds a hash table on (person, week) and probes it. That’s O(N).
Visually, for one person’s timeline:
Week     | Employer | LAG() sees      | Self-join matches
---------|----------|-----------------|-------------------
Week 1   | A        | NULL            | no match (no Week 0)
Week 2   | A        | A (from Wk 1)   | A (from Wk 1)
Week 3   | B        | A (from Wk 2)   | A (from Wk 2)  ← SWITCH
Week 4   | B        | B (from Wk 3)   | B (from Wk 3)
Both detect the A → B switch at Week 3.






NoteWhen can you use a self-join instead of LAG()?



The self-join works when the lag has a known, fixed time offset — here, exactly 7 days. If your data has irregular spacing (some people have records every week, others every fortnight), the self-join will miss non-standard gaps. LAG() always looks back one row regardless of the time gap. For STP, where records are weekly by construction, they give the same result.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
text:
The pattern: chunk by person
The general recipe for window functions on person-week PLIDA data:
library(duckdb); library(DBI); library(dplyr); library(dbplyr)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = "my.duckdb", read_only = FALSE)
dbExecute(con, "SET memory_limit = '20GB'")

n_buckets <- 10

# Create the output table
dbExecute(con, "CREATE TABLE switches (
  SYNTHETIC_AEUID VARCHAR, WEEK_ENDING DATE,
  new_employer VARCHAR, prev_employer VARCHAR)")

for (b in 0:(n_buckets - 1)) {
  dbExecute(con, glue::glue("
    INSERT INTO switches
    WITH lagged AS (
      SELECT *, LAG(ABN_HASH_TRUNC) OVER (
        PARTITION BY SYNTHETIC_AEUID ORDER BY WEEK_ENDING
      ) AS prev_employer
      FROM stp WHERE hash(SYNTHETIC_AEUID) % {n_buckets} = {b}
    )
    SELECT SYNTHETIC_AEUID, WEEK_ENDING,
           ABN_HASH_TRUNC, prev_employer
    FROM lagged
    WHERE prev_employer IS NOT NULL
      AND ABN_HASH_TRUNC != prev_employer
  "))
}
The hash(...) % n_buckets filter means each iteration processes only 1/n of the people. Because the window function is PARTITION BY person, each person’s partition is entirely within one bucket — no cross-bucket issues, no edge effects.






TipChoosing the number of buckets



The goal is to keep each bucket small enough that the window function’s intermediate state fits in your memory_limit. On this machine: 700M rows in 10 buckets = 70M rows per bucket, which fits in 24 GB. A safe rule of thumb: aim for 50–100M rows per bucket. More buckets = more iterations but safer memory. Fewer = fewer iterations but closer to the limit.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
text:
What about ASOF joins?
For the cross-dataset version of this problem — “find the most recent health service before each job switch” — DuckDB’s ASOF join is often better than windowing both tables and then joining. It directly finds the nearest temporal match:
SELECT s.*, h.services, h.fees
FROM switches s
ASOF JOIN mbs_monthly h
  ON s.SYNTHETIC_AEUID = h.SYNTHETIC_AEUID
  AND s.WEEK_ENDING >= h.month;
This avoids computing a windowed timeline on the MBS side entirely. See the DuckDB ASOF join docs for the full syntax.

title: Window function strategies on billion-row data
text:
Beyond DuckDB
For production pipelines that need to process this data daily, the next step beyond chunked DuckDB is a custom streaming processor in Rust called from R via extendr. A reader that walks sorted parquet files and maintains (current_person, previous_employer) state uses O(1) memory and runs at disk speed — no sorting, no hash tables, no chunking. This is overkill for exploratory analysis but the right tool for a pipeline that runs on every data refresh.

text:
The join model
The spine files are the PLIDA-specific glue. Each dataset provider (ABS, ATO, DHDA) has its own person identifier (SYNTHETIC_AEUID), and the *-spine.csv tables map each provider’s identifier back to a common spine_id. Demographic tables are keyed directly by SPINE_ID.
So to ask “how much did MBS spend per service, by sex and age, for people born between 1960 and 1970”, we need:
demo[SPINE_ID]
  ⟕ dhda_spine[spine_id, SYNTHETIC_AEUID]
  ⟕ mbs[SYNTHETIC_AEUID, NUMSERV, BENPAID]
This is the canonical three-table join we benchmark in T3.

title: The data
section: Why this is a larger-than-memory problem
text:
Why this is a larger-than-memory problem
The MBS 2015 claims file on its own is 44.5 GB CSV, which at standard R read rates would occupy far more than the 32 GB of RAM the machine has. Even compressed to parquet the file is about 18.7 GB — still more than half of physical RAM, and enough to evict everything else when you materialise it as a tibble. Any approach that relies on loading the full file into memory is therefore a non-starter, and the benchmarks in later pages treat dplyr/data.table on MBS as “skipped (OOM)” by construction.

title: Using PLIDA: larger-than-memory data in R
text:
WarningDraft



This site is a work in progress. The code, benchmarks, and recommendations are still being tested and may change. Don’t rely on anything here yet.

title: Using PLIDA: larger-than-memory data in R
text:
What this site is
A worked demonstration of modern R data-engineering tools — arrow, duckdb / dbplyr, data.table, and base dplyr — applied to PLIDA-style administrative microdata that is much larger than the machine’s RAM. All code runs on a 32 GB MacBook against a ~750 GB synthetic PLIDA corpus produced by the fplida package.
Each main chapter describes one piece of the larger-than-memory workflow and shows the recommended way to do it. The evidence behind the recommendations — the timings, memory footprints, and the things we tried that did not work — lives in the benchmark appendices at the back of the book. Read the main chapters for the what and how; dip into the appendices when you want the why.

title: Using PLIDA: larger-than-memory data in R
text:
The short answer
For PLIDA-style data on a machine with 32 GB of RAM:

Store raw data as parquet. Convert CSV → parquet with DuckDB’s read_csv_auto() piped into COPY ... TO parquet. It is roughly 2× faster than the Arrow equivalent on this corpus and writes slightly smaller files. Keep zstd compression, medium row groups (ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000), and medium-sized output files. See A1.
Put the parquet into a materialised DuckDB database once the workload becomes repetitive or join-heavy. Set memory_limit explicitly (~60% of physical RAM). Views over parquet are fine for one-off exploration. See A1.
Query the database through dbplyr. Compose dplyr verbs against tbl(con, "name") and call collect() only on the final small result. Tidyverse all the way — raw SQL is almost never needed. See A2.
Use filtering joins (semi_join, anti_join) instead of filter(x %in% ...) when the right-hand side is large. Push filter() upstream of every join. Expect DuckDB to handle 100M+ row joins that Arrow cannot finish on a 32 GB machine. See A3.
For window functions on billion-row panel data, chunk by a person-hash bucket. A single LAG() over 700M STP rows does not fit; ten independent calls over 70M-row buckets complete in about 20 seconds. See A4.

The rest of this site walks through each of those recommendations in turn.

title: Recommendations
text:
How the pages fit together

The data — what the fplida corpus looks like, which tables we use, and the join model.
CSV to parquet — how arrow streams a 44.5 GB CSV through a 2 GB memory footprint, and the cost/benefit of the conversion.
DuckDB — two ways to wire parquet up to DuckDB (views vs materialised tables), and the build cost of each.
Benchmarks — five backends, three canonical tasks, timings and peak RSS.
Recommendations — the rule of thumb, with caveats.

title: Using PLIDA: larger-than-memory data in R
text:
Reproducibility
All source data lives outside this repository at /Users/willmackey/offline/fplida-data/fplida_30m_csv/ (synthetic, produced by fplida). All intermediate parquet and DuckDB files live at /Users/willmackey/offline/using-plida-data/ so the project folder stays small. The scripts in R/ are numbered in dependency order; R/01-convert-csv-to-parquet.R runs first, then R/01b-convert-supplementary.R, R/02-build-duckdb.R, and finally R/04-run-benchmarks.R.

title: Case study 1: How much do people earn?
text: This case study asks the most boring useful question you can ask of the individual tax returns: what does the distribution of taxable income look like, and how has it moved over time? It uses one table — itr_inc, the ATO income sub-table — and a few dplyr verbs. If you’re picking up PLIDA for the first time, start here.

title: Case study 1: How much do people earn?
text:
Read the data with duckplyr
duckplyr can read parquet files directly — no DBI connection, no database setup. Just point it at the files:


Code
itr <- read_parquet_duckdb(
  file.path(paths$parquet_root, "itr_context/*.parquet"),
  prudence = "stingy"
)


itr is now a lazy duckplyr frame backed by DuckDB. Nothing has been read into memory. You work with it using normal dplyr verbs — but the computation happens in DuckDB.






NoteWhat is prudence = "stingy"?



By default, duckplyr will materialise results into R memory when you access them. Setting prudence = "stingy" prevents this — it keeps everything in DuckDB until you explicitly collect(). This is what you want for large data: it ensures you never accidentally pull 170 million rows into R.




Code
glimpse(itr)


Rows: ??
Columns: 6
$ SYNTHETIC_AEUID          <chr> "000000000200", "0000000002D7", "0000000004FB…
$ INCM_YR                  <dbl> 2011, 2011, 2011, 2011, 2011, 2011, 2011, 201…
$ OCPTN_GRP_CD             <dbl> 3114, 2521, 5614, 7219, 7129, 2343, 2322, 612…
$ SUB_OCPTN_GRP_CD         <dbl> 311412, 252112, 561411, 721911, 712916, 23431…
$ MN_BUS_TAX_OFC_ANZSIC_CD <dbl> 5, 14, 10, 3, 15, 17, 12, 7, 13, 17, 16, 16, …
$ CLNT_RSDNT_IND           <chr> "Y", "Y", "Y", "Y", "Y", "Y", "Y", "Y", "Y", …


Six columns, about 170 million rows across all financial years. INCM_YR is the financial year and OCPTN_GRP_CD is a 4-digit ANZSCO occupation code. This context table doesn’t have taxable income directly — a common PLIDA experience: the column you want is in a different file. But we can do plenty with what we have.

title: Case study 1: How much do people earn?
section: Count taxpayers by occupation group over time
text:
Count taxpayers by occupation group over time
How many people file a tax return each year, broken down by ANZSCO major group (the first digit of the occupation code)?


Code
tic("Groupby on 170M rows")
occ_by_year <- itr |>
  filter(!is.na(ocptn_grp_cd)) |>
  mutate(occ_major = as.integer(floor(ocptn_grp_cd / 1000))) |>
  count(incm_yr, occ_major) |>
  collect()
toc()


Groupby on 170M rows: 0.275 sec elapsed


Code
occ_by_year



  



Everything up to collect() is lazy — the dplyr chain builds a SQL query that DuckDB executes. 170 million input rows collapse to a few hundred output rows. That is the pattern: push the heavy work to DuckDB, collect() only when the result fits in memory.






TipThe golden rule



collect() only when the result is small. If you find yourself wanting to collect() and then filter(), you have it backwards.








WarningWhy floor(x / 1000) and not x %/% 1000 or as.integer(x / 1000)?



Both alternatives look equivalent to floor() but bite in different ways. %/% is unreliable through Arrow’s translator (fine in DuckDB, but using the same code across engines is the point). as.integer(x / 1000) is actively wrong across engines: R and Arrow truncate toward zero, but DuckDB’s CAST(... AS INTEGER) rounds half‑to‑even, so as.integer(192.6) = 192 in R but 193 in DuckDB. floor() is portable and obvious. See Data manipulation § The as.integer() trap for the full story.

title: Case study 1: How much do people earn?
text:
Plot
Once you’ve collect()ed, you’re back in normal R-land.


Code
occ_labels <- c(
  "1" = "Managers", "2" = "Professionals", "3" = "Technicians",
  "4" = "Community/personal", "5" = "Clerical/admin",
  "6" = "Sales", "7" = "Machinery operators", "8" = "Labourers"
)

occ_by_year |>
  filter(occ_major %in% 1:8) |>
  mutate(occ_label = occ_labels[as.character(occ_major)]) |>
  ggplot(aes(incm_yr, n, colour = occ_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_comma()) +
  labs(x = "Income year", y = "Tax returns filed",
       colour = "ANZSCO major group",
       title = "Tax returns by occupation group over time",
       subtitle = "fplida synthetic data, 30M person corpus") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

title: Case study 1: How much do people earn?
text:
What just happened
You aggregated 170 million rows twice and plotted both results — without loading the data into R memory. The same operation with read_parquet() and dplyr-on-tibbles takes about 35 seconds and 14.5 GB of RAM (see A2). On a smaller machine it would crash.






ImportantThe comparison that matters



The same groupby on 170M rows: dplyr-on-tibbles takes 35 seconds and 14.5 GB of RAM. dbplyr over DuckDB takes under a second and uses negligible memory. See the full benchmark results.


The workflow is always:

Open a connection, grab a lazy tbl().
Build the query with dplyr verbs.
collect() the (small) result into R.
ggplot2 from there.

title: Case study 1: How much do people earn?
section: duckplyr vs dbplyr: when to use which
text:
duckplyr vs dbplyr: when to use which
This case study uses duckplyr — it reads parquet directly and runs dplyr verbs through DuckDB with no database setup. For single-table operations like this, duckplyr is the simplest tool.
For joins across large tables (case studies 2–4), we switch to dbplyr with a DBI connection to a materialised DuckDB database. The reason: duckplyr’s in-memory DuckDB engine runs out of memory on joins involving 30M+ rows per side on a 32 GB machine. A direct DBI connection to a .duckdb file lets DuckDB operate on its native storage with efficient buffer-pool paging — a fundamentally different memory profile. See case study 2 for that approach.






NoteThe rule of thumb



duckplyr for single-table work (filter, mutate, groupby, summarise) — no setup, reads parquet directly, handles 170M rows easily. dbplyr + DBI for multi-table joins and window functions at scale — needs a materialised DuckDB database but handles operations duckplyr can’t.

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
This case study asks: how does MBS service use vary by sex and age? It’s the simplest genuinely useful PLIDA question that requires joining across datasets — demographics from one source, health service use from another, linked via the spine.
The MBS 2015 claims file has 372 million rows. Loading it into memory is not an option on a 32 GB machine — we tested that in the join benchmarks, and even arrow’s join engine was killed by the operating system. DuckDB handles the whole thing in a few seconds.
This is the standard recipe from the Joins chapter: a read-only connection to the materialised DuckDB database, three lazy tbl() handles, dbplyr verbs, collect() at the end. Everything else that follows in this case study is just choosing the right joins and pushing filters to the right place.

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
The PLIDA join model
PLIDA data comes from different government agencies, and each agency has its own person identifier (SYNTHETIC_AEUID). The spine tables link them: each spine maps spine_id (a universal key) to one agency’s identifier.
So the join chain for “demographics → MBS” is:
demo[SPINE_ID]  →  dhda_spine[spine_id, SYNTHETIC_AEUID]  →  mbs[SYNTHETIC_AEUID]






NoteWhy the spine tables exist



No single agency holds a universal person identifier. The spine tables are the linkage layer — they map each agency’s identifier to a common spine_id. You’ll always need at least one spine join to link data across agencies.

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
Set up the lazy tables


Code
demo       <- tbl(con, "demo")       |> rename_with(tolower)
dhda_spine <- tbl(con, "dhda_spine") |> rename_with(tolower)
mbs        <- tbl(con, "mbs")        |> rename_with(tolower)


Three handles. Nothing loaded. rename_with(tolower) pushes every column to snake_case as it comes out of DuckDB, so the rest of the pipe can stay lowercase. Let’s check what we’re working with:


Code
demo |> head(3) |> collect()



  



Code
dhda_spine |> head(3) |> collect()



  



Code
mbs |> head(3) |> collect()

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
Things to notice
Normalise column case up front. rename_with(tolower) at the top of each tbl() means the rest of the pipe joins on spine_id and synthetic_aeuid without any one-off renames. That is the idiom the book uses throughout (Data manipulation § The mental model).
select() before joining. We select only the columns we need from each table before the join. DuckDB pushes the projection down, so it reads fewer columns from disk and the hash table is smaller.
Filters also go before joins. The age_2015 >= 0 filter is on the already-joined result here because the column only exists after the mutate, but where a filter exists on a raw column (e.g. year_of_birth >= 1960), push it upstream of every join. Arrow’s optimiser will not do this transformation for you; see Joins § The habit that matters most.






Importantsemi_join() / anti_join() instead of filter(x %in% ...)



Not used here, but worth calling out because it is the single most useful join idiom for PLIDA work. If you ever find yourself writing filter(synthetic_aeuid %in% (other_table |> pull(synthetic_aeuid))), rewrite it as semi_join(other_table, by = "synthetic_aeuid"). The %in% form silently materialises the right‑hand column into R memory and ships a giant IN (...) literal back in SQL; the semi_join() keeps the whole operation inside DuckDB. See Joins § filtering joins.








Warningcollect() comes last — not in the middle



If you accidentally write demo |> collect() |> inner_join(dhda_spine, ...), you’ve loaded 30 million rows into R memory before joining — and the join to MBS would try to pull 372 million rows in too. Keep the pipe lazy until the result is aggregated down to something small.

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
Plot


Code
result |>
  filter(core_gender %in% c("M", "F")) |>
  ggplot(aes(age_group, services / 1e6, fill = core_gender)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_comma(suffix = "M")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("F" = "#E69F00", "M" = "#56B4E9"),
                    labels = c("F" = "Female", "M" = "Male")) +
  labs(x = "Age group", y = "MBS services (millions)",
       fill = NULL,
       title = "MBS service use by age and sex, 2015",
       subtitle = "fplida synthetic data, 30M person corpus") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)











Code
result |>
  filter(core_gender %in% c("M", "F")) |>
  mutate(fee_per_service = fee_charged / services) |>
  ggplot(aes(age_group, fee_per_service, colour = core_gender)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("F" = "#E69F00", "M" = "#56B4E9"),
                      labels = c("F" = "Female", "M" = "Male")) +
  labs(x = "Age group", y = "Average fee per service",
       colour = NULL,
       title = "Average MBS fee per service by age and sex",
       subtitle = "fplida synthetic data") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
When dbplyr is not enough
For the vast majority of PLIDA questions, dplyr verbs translate cleanly. When they do not — typically a DuckDB-specific function with no tidy counterpart — the lightest escape is a single sql() fragment inside a mutate() or filter():
demo |>
  mutate(something = sql("SOME_DUCKDB_FUNCTION(col)"))
You stay in the dplyr pipe and hand DuckDB a literal expression for that one column. Reserve it for the genuinely irreducible case; if more than one sql(...) appears in a pipeline, rewrite the tidyverse side instead. dbGetQuery() with a hand-written SQL query is almost never the right answer — it is used once in the site for ASOF JOIN and not elsewhere.

title: Case study 2: Who uses health services?
text:
A note on table layout
We found in the storage-layout join experiments that sorting MBS by the join key makes point lookups 47× faster but doesn’t speed up broad analytical queries like this one. If you’re looking up one specific person’s claims, sort the table. For population-level analysis, it doesn’t matter.
The one thing that helps every join: using an integer hash key instead of the string AEUID. We measured a 2× speedup.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
text:
This case study is the hard one. We ask: do people who experience a spike in health service use switch employers at higher rates? It requires:
Each step involves window operations on hundreds of millions of rows, partitioned by person and ordered by date. As we found in the window-function benchmarks, DuckDB’s window functions OOM on 700M+ rows on a 32 GB machine — so we chunk by person-hash bucket.
Everything below is written in tidyverse R. The one exception is the CREATE VIEW calls that point DuckDB at the parquet files — that is one-off setup DDL with no dbplyr equivalent, and we wrap it in a helper.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
text:
The strategy
We tested two full approaches end-to-end:

Materialise-then-query: load STP and MBS from parquet into a DuckDB working database, then run the window functions against native DuckDB tables. Total time: ~235 seconds.
Views over parquet: create DuckDB views pointing at the parquet files, then run the window functions reading parquet directly. Total time: ~75 seconds.

Views win by 3×. The per-query cost is slightly higher (parquet decode on each bucket), but skipping the 170-second materialisation step more than compensates. In both cases the window functions are chunked by person hash bucket — neither lag() nor a self-join can handle 700M rows in one shot on 32 GB.






ImportantThree rules for window functions at PLIDA scale




Chunk by person hash bucket. ~70M rows per bucket is comfortable on 32 GB. 700M is not.
Pre-aggregate before windowing. Person-months, not individual claims.
Use views over parquet rather than materialising into DuckDB — unless you will query the same table many times.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
text:
Connect to the data


Code
db_path <- file.path(paths$duckdb_dir, "cs04_working.duckdb")
if (file_exists(db_path)) file_delete(db_path)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = db_path, read_only = FALSE)
invisible(dbExecute(con, "SET threads TO 8"))
invisible(dbExecute(con, "SET memory_limit = '20GB'"))
tmp_dir <- file.path(paths$offline_root, "duckdb_tmp")
dir_create(tmp_dir)
invisible(dbExecute(con, glue("SET temp_directory = '{tmp_dir}'")))

pq <- paths$parquet_root

# Small helper: the one piece of SQL in this case study. CREATE VIEW has
# no dbplyr equivalent — we hide it behind register_view() and never look
# at it again.
register_view <- function(con, name, glob) {
  dbExecute(con, glue(
    "CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW {name} AS SELECT * FROM read_parquet('{glob}')"
  ))
  invisible(NULL)
}

tic("Register views over parquet")

# STP gets an extra `bucket` column (0-9) computed once in the view.
# We use this downstream to process one tenth of the people at a time.
# Doing the same arithmetic inside the dbplyr pipeline on every
# iteration confuses DuckDB's chunk-skipping, so the one-off view is
# the right place to compute it.
dbExecute(con, glue("CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW stp AS
  SELECT *, CAST(hash(SYNTHETIC_AEUID) % 10 AS INTEGER) AS bucket
  FROM read_parquet('{pq}/stp/stp-fy2122*.parquet')
  WHERE ABN_HASH_TRUNC IS NOT NULL"))


[1] 0


Code
register_view(con, "mbs",        glue("{pq}/dhda-mbs/*.parquet"))
register_view(con, "ato_spine",  glue("{pq}/ato-spine/*.parquet"))
register_view(con, "dhda_spine", glue("{pq}/dhda-spine/*.parquet"))
register_view(con, "demo",       glue("{pq}/demo/*.parquet"))
toc()


Register views over parquet: 0.031 sec elapsed


Code
stp        <- tbl(con, "stp")        |> rename_with(tolower)
mbs        <- tbl(con, "mbs")        |> rename_with(tolower)
ato_spine  <- tbl(con, "ato_spine")  |> rename_with(tolower)
dhda_spine <- tbl(con, "dhda_spine") |> rename_with(tolower)


Views are instant — DuckDB just notes the parquet path. The 697 million STP rows and 372 million MBS rows stay on disk until a query needs them. Every step from here on is plain dplyr.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
section: Step 1: Derive employer switches from STP (chunked)

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
section: Step 2: Derive health shocks from MBS
text:
Step 2: Derive health shocks from MBS
A “health shock” is a month where someone’s MBS service count is at least double their trailing 3-month average. Two steps: collapse 372M claims to person-months, then apply a rolling window on the smaller table.


Code
tic("Collapse MBS to person-months")
mbs_monthly <- mbs |>
  mutate(month = floor_date(dos, "month")) |>
  group_by(synthetic_aeuid, month) |>
  summarise(services = sum(numserv,    na.rm = TRUE),
            fees     = sum(feecharged, na.rm = TRUE),
            claims   = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  compute(name = "mbs_monthly", temporary = FALSE, overwrite = TRUE)
toc()


Collapse MBS to person-months: 20.62 sec elapsed


Code
cat("Person-months:", comma(mbs_monthly |> summarise(n = n()) |> pull(n)), "\n")


Person-months: 206,131,399 


floor_date(dos, "month") rounds each service date down to the first of its month. summarise() aggregates claims per person per month. compute() writes the result out to a persistent table so the rolling window in the next step doesn’t have to redo this group-by.
Now the rolling window: for each person, compute the mean number of services over the previous three months (not counting the current month), and flag months where services are at least double that trailing mean.


Code
tic("Flag health shocks (rolling window on person-months)")
mbs_shocks <- mbs_monthly |>
  group_by(synthetic_aeuid) |>
  window_order(month) |>
  window_frame(-3, -1) |>                     # ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING
  mutate(trailing_avg = mean(services, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!is.na(trailing_avg),
         trailing_avg > 0,
         services >= 2 * trailing_avg) |>
  transmute(synthetic_aeuid,
            shock_month = month,
            services, trailing_avg) |>
  compute(name = "mbs_shocks", temporary = FALSE, overwrite = TRUE)
toc()


Flag health shocks (rolling window on person-months): 10.039 sec elapsed


Code
n_shocks  <- mbs_shocks |> summarise(n = n()) |> pull(n)
n_shocked <- mbs_shocks |> summarise(n = n_distinct(synthetic_aeuid)) |> pull(n)
cat("Health shock events:", comma(n_shocks),
    "across", comma(n_shocked), "people\n")


Health shock events: 29,893,469 across 20,343,421 people


window_frame(from, to) says which rows to include in the rolling window: -3 means three rows back, -1 means one row back. So window_frame(-3, -1) means “look at rows 3, 2, and 1 rows before the current row.” Negative = earlier, positive = later, 0 = the current row. Everything else is ordinary group_by() |> window_order() |> mutate().






NoteAggregate first, then window



Collapsing 372M claims to ~206M person-months before windowing is what makes this step work at all. 206M rows fit in memory for the sort. Running the rolling window directly on 372M raw claims would hit the same memory limit we hit with STP.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
section: Step 3: Link switches to shocks via spine

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
text:
Plot


Code
tic("Baseline switching rate")
baseline <- stp_switches |>
  mutate(switch_month = floor_date(week_ending, "month")) |>
  group_by(switch_month) |>
  summarise(switches = n(),
            people   = n_distinct(synthetic_aeuid),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(switch_month) |>
  collect()
toc()


Baseline switching rate: 0.026 sec elapsed




Code
if (nrow(baseline) > 0 && nrow(post_shock) > 0) {
  bind_rows(
    baseline   |> mutate(group = "All switchers",
                         month = as.Date(switch_month),
                         n = switches) |> select(month, n, group),
    post_shock |> mutate(group = "Post health-shock (3-month window)",
                         month = as.Date(switch_month),
                         n = switches_post_shock) |> select(month, n, group)
  ) |>
    ggplot(aes(month, n, colour = group)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = label_comma()) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Employer switches", colour = NULL,
         title = "Employer switches: post-health-shock vs all workers",
         subtitle = "STP FY2122 x MBS 2015, fplida synthetic data") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
} else {
  cat("No switches detected — expected with synthetic data that does not model job mobility.\n")
  cat("The code, strategy, and timings above are the deliverable of this case study.\n")
}


No switches detected — expected with synthetic data that does not model job mobility.
The code, strategy, and timings above are the deliverable of this case study.








NoteSynthetic data limitation



The fplida synthetic data does not model job switching — each person keeps the same employer throughout. So the switch count is 0. The pipeline design, memory management, and timings are real; the results would be meaningful on actual PLIDA data where people do change jobs.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
text:
What it cost










Step
Approach
Time




Register views over parquet
CREATE VIEW … read_parquet()
instant


Derive employer switches (STP)
Chunked lag(), 10 person-hash buckets
~45s


Collapse MBS to person-months
mutate(floor_date) + summarise()
~20s


Flag health shocks
window_frame(-3, -1) + mean()
~10s


Cross-spine join: switches x shocks
Two inner_join()s via crosswalk
<1s





Total: about 75 seconds. None of the steps exceeded 20 GB of RAM. The same pipeline against materialised DuckDB tables takes ~235 seconds (the 170-second load dominates). Views over parquet are the right default when you’re running the pipeline once; materialise only if you’ll re-query the same table many times.

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
text:
Why we chose this approach
We tried four approaches before settling on views + chunked lag(). All of them — and the reason each failed or succeeded — are documented in the window-function benchmarks:

lag() on 700M rows: out of memory (sort needs too much space)
Self-join on 700M rows: out of memory (lookup structure too big)
lag() on 70M rows (one bucket): 2.3 seconds, success
Self-join on 70M rows: 2.0 seconds, success
Chunked self-join, 10 buckets: 20 seconds, success
Chunked lag() over parquet views, 10 buckets: 45 seconds, success
Views + chunked lag() via dbplyr: 75 seconds end-to-end, best total time

title: Case study 4: Job switching and health shocks
section: When SQL is clearer than dplyr
text:
When SQL is clearer than dplyr
The window-function steps use raw SQL via dbExecute(). This is deliberate:

ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING has no natural dplyr equivalent.
DATE_TRUNC() is a DuckDB function with no dplyr translation.
The chunking loop with glue::glue() is cleaner in SQL.

The downstream aggregation and plotting go back to dplyr/dbplyr. Use each tool where it’s strongest.

title: Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
text:
The first two case studies assumed you already had parquet files and a DuckDB database ready to go. This one starts from scratch: raw CSVs, no intermediate files, nothing pre-built. By the end you will have a chart on screen.
This is what you would actually do on your first day with a new PLIDA extract.

title: Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
section: Step 1: Look at what you have
text:
Step 1: Look at what you have
You have been given a directory of CSVs. Before doing anything clever, just look at them.


Code
csv_root <- paths$csv_root
tibble(
  dataset = dir_ls(csv_root, type = "directory") |> path_file(),
  n_files = vapply(dir_ls(csv_root, type = "directory"), \(d) length(dir_ls(d, glob = "*.csv")), integer(1)),
  total_gb = vapply(dir_ls(csv_root, type = "directory"), path_size_gb, double(1)) |> round(1)
) |>
  arrange(desc(total_gb)) |>
  head(10)



  



751 GB of CSV across 34 datasets. On a 32 GB machine, you are not reading any of this into R with read_csv(). Don’t even try.






WarningDo not read_csv() a PLIDA file



It is tempting. You have done it a thousand times on smaller data. But a single MBS year file is 44.5 GB — far more than your machine’s 32 GB of RAM. Even a “medium” ITR file will eat several GB and slow everything else down. Use DuckDB’s streaming CSV reader instead (next step).

title: Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
section: Step 2: Convert the CSVs you need to parquet
text:
Step 2: Convert the CSVs you need to parquet
You almost certainly do not need all 34 datasets. Pick the ones relevant to your question and convert them. We covered why parquet is better than CSV in the Reading and storing chapter — the short version is: smaller, faster, typed, and columnar.
The recommended converter is DuckDB’s streaming CSV reader piped into COPY ... TO parquet. On the six‑file benchmark in A1 it was about 2× faster than Arrow for this step, wrote slightly smaller parquet, and stayed under 3 GB of RAM on a 44.5 GB source file.
Here is the recipe for one file:
library(duckdb); library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb())
dbExecute(con, "SET threads TO 8")
dbExecute(con, "SET memory_limit = '20GB'")
dbExecute(con, "SET preserve_insertion_order = false")

dbExecute(con, "
  COPY (
    SELECT * FROM read_csv_auto('path/to/big-file.csv')
  ) TO 'output/dir/'
  (FORMAT PARQUET, COMPRESSION zstd,
   ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000, ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20)
")

dbDisconnect(con, shutdown = TRUE)
Five details worth knowing:

read_csv_auto() is streaming. DuckDB never materialises the whole CSV in memory.
COMPRESSION zstd is worth setting explicitly — it shaves a meaningful fraction off the output (e.g. 1.82 GB → 1.22 GB on one of our reference tables).
ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000 is the right middle ground. Tiny row groups (10k) make full scans multiples slower.
ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20 gives about 5 million rows per output file. Many medium‑sized files parallelise far better than one enormous one.
preserve_insertion_order = false is a DuckDB pragma worth flipping for bulk export where row order does not matter.







TipHow the streaming works



DuckDB’s CSV reader operates on batches of rows, pushes them through any SELECT projection, and streams each batch straight to the parquet writer. A 44.5 GB input passes through about 2–3 GB of RAM; the memory budget does not grow with the file. The full measurement is in A1: peak RSS stays flat regardless of input size.


Do this for each dataset you need. It is boring but fast, and you only do it once.

title: Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
text:
Step 3: Build a DuckDB database
With the parquet files in place, you now have a choice. You can query the parquet directly (with either arrow or DuckDB views), or you can load the parquet into a materialised DuckDB database. We tested both in Reading and storing and in the join benchmarks, and the answer is clear: materialise if you are going to query the data more than a couple of times.
library(duckdb); library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = "my_project.duckdb", read_only = FALSE)

# DuckDB will use up to 20 GB for the ingest. On a 32 GB machine this
# leaves room for R, the OS, and the rest of the world.
dbExecute(con, "SET memory_limit = '20GB'")
dbExecute(con, "SET threads TO 8")

# One table per parquet directory. DuckDB reads the glob.
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE TABLE demo AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('parquet/demo/*.parquet')
")

dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE TABLE mbs AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('parquet/dhda-mbs/*.parquet')
  ORDER BY SYNTHETIC_AEUID
")
Notice the ORDER BY on the MBS table. We showed in the storage-variant experiments that sorting a fact table by its join key makes point lookups 47× faster. It costs a couple of extra minutes at build time and pays off on every subsequent query that filters by the join key.






NoteWhen sorting helps (and when it doesn’t)



Sorting by the join key is a big win for point lookups (looking up one person’s data: 47× faster) and a modest help for narrow filtered joins when the filter correlates with the key. It does not help full-table-scan joins where you touch every row anyway — see the storage-variant experiments for the honest numbers.


If you also want faster joins across the board, add a hashed integer key at ingest time:
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE TABLE mbs AS
  SELECT hash(SYNTHETIC_AEUID) AS aeuid_hash, *
  FROM read_parquet('parquet/dhda-mbs/*.parquet')
  ORDER BY aeuid_hash
")
Integer joins are about 2× faster than string joins. Keep the original SYNTHETIC_AEUID for display; use the hash for joining.

title: Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
text:
Step 5: Plot


Code
result |>
  filter(CORE_GENDER %in% c("M", "F"),
         BTOS %in% c("GP", "SP", "P", "O")) |>
  mutate(BTOS = recode(BTOS,
    "GP" = "GP", "SP" = "Specialist", "P" = "Pathology", "O" = "Other")) |>
  ggplot(aes(age_group, services / 1e6, fill = BTOS)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~ CORE_GENDER, labeller = labeller(
    CORE_GENDER = c("F" = "Female", "M" = "Male"))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_comma(suffix = "M")) +
  labs(x = "Age group", y = "MBS services (millions)",
       fill = "Service type",
       title = "MBS service use by age, sex, and service type, 2015",
       subtitle = "fplida synthetic data") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

The whole pipeline, summarised

Total wall time for the one-off setup (steps 1–2): maybe 10–15 minutes for a realistic subset of PLIDA. After that, every query is seconds. If you are used to waiting minutes for read_csv() to load a file — or crashing R entirely — this is a different world.

title: Case study 3: From CSV to chart in 20 minutes
text:
Common mistakes






ImportantThe four things that crash your machine



These are the mistakes that will freeze a 32 GB laptop. Every one of them involves accidentally pulling too much data into R memory. The fix is always the same: push the filtering and aggregation to DuckDB, collect() only the small result.


A few things that will bite you if you are not expecting them:
Calling collect() too early. If you write mbs |> collect() |> filter(...), you have just tried to load 372 million rows into R memory. Your machine will freeze. Always filter before collecting. The rule is: collect() goes at the end, and the result should be small.
Forgetting read_only = TRUE. By default, DuckDB locks the file for exclusive access. If you open it in your R session and then try to render a Quarto doc that also opens it, one of them will fail. Always pass read_only = TRUE when you are not writing.
Not setting memory_limit. DuckDB will cheerfully try to use all your RAM for a big sort or join. On a 32 GB machine, set memory_limit = '20GB' so the OS and R can breathe.
One enormous parquet file. If you write 372 million rows to a single parquet file, DuckDB cannot parallelise the read and zonemaps are less useful. Set ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20 (≈5 million rows per file) in the COPY ... TO parquet step.

title: DuckDB: views vs materialised tables
text:
Once the parquet files exist, there are two natural ways to turn them into a DuckDB database:


library(duckdb); library(DBI)
con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = "plida_views.duckdb")
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW mbs AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/offline/.../dhda-mbs/*.parquet')
")
Nothing is copied. DuckDB builds a view that points at the parquet glob and re-reads it on every query. Build cost is effectively zero. Every query pays the parquet decode cost, but DuckDB pushes filters and projections down to the reader, so for well-predicated queries the pay-as-you-go cost is modest.



con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = "plida_tables.duckdb")
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE mbs AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/offline/.../dhda-mbs/*.parquet')
")
The parquet is read once and written into DuckDB’s own columnar storage. Subsequent queries never touch parquet at all. Build cost is linear in input size; query cost is usually lower than views and much more predictable because DuckDB now owns the storage format, the zonemaps, and the compression.

title: DuckDB: views vs materialised tables
text:
Once the parquet files exist, there are two natural ways to turn them into a DuckDB database:

con <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = "plida_views.duckdb")
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW mbs AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('/offline/.../dhda-mbs/*.parquet')

Every query pays the parquet decode cost, but DuckDB pushes filters and projections down to the reader, so for well-predicated queries the pay-as-you-go cost is modest.

dbExecute(con, "

title: DuckDB: views vs materialised tables
DuckDB: views vs materialised tables
section: When to prefer views, when to prefer tables
text:
When to prefer views, when to prefer tables
The fresh benchmarks on this machine make the trade-off a little more specific than the generic rule:

On single-table scans and groupbys, views over parquet are already excellent. In the current benchmark run the view was slightly faster than the materialised table on T1 and T2, and the difference was tiny in absolute terms.
On join-heavy work, tables pull away. On the 3-table MBS join (T3), the materialised table ran in 3.71s; the view-over-parquet version took 6.39s.

So the practical rule is: use views for ad-hoc exploration and one-off scans; use tables once the workload becomes join-heavy, window-heavy, or repetitive enough that the parquet decode cost keeps coming back. Prefer views if the underlying parquet is changing, if disk space is precious (tables duplicate the storage in exchange for speed), or if you want other non-DuckDB tools to consume the same files unchanged.
For a personal research project where parquet is the canonical source and DuckDB is just the query engine, I use tables for anything I am going to run many times (the main analysis tables) and views for supplementary stuff I touch once.

title: Recommendations
text:
On larger-than-memory PLIDA data, on a laptop or workstation with modest RAM:

Convert CSV → parquet with DuckDB (read_csv_auto + COPY ... TO parquet). On the local six-file large-file benchmark, DuckDB was about 2x faster than Arrow for this step while writing slightly smaller parquet. Keep zstd compression, medium row groups (ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000), and medium-sized files (ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20 landed well here).
Partition only where the filter pattern justifies it. On the local layout benchmark, partitioning itr_context by INCM_YR improved year-filter queries, but the gain was small in absolute terms on local NVMe and irrelevant for one-year tables like MBS 2015. Partition multi-year tables on columns you really do filter on all the time; do not over-partition everything out of habit.
Load parquet into a DuckDB database as materialised tables once the workload becomes join-heavy, window-heavy, or repetitive. Use SET memory_limit='20GB' (or whatever leaves the OS and other processes room to breathe) and SET threads TO 8. For one-off scans and light exploration, views over parquet are fine.
Query through dbplyr by default. Use tbl(con, "name"), compose dplyr verbs, and only collect() when the result is small enough for R memory. Drop into raw SQL via sql(...) inside mutate() / filter() or use dbGetQuery() when the dplyr translation is missing or awkward.

That’s it. Everything else on this site is evidence that this recipe works.

title: Recommendations
text:
On larger-than-memory PLIDA data, on a laptop or workstation with modest RAM:

Convert CSV → parquet with DuckDB (read_csv_auto + COPY ... TO parquet). On the local six-file large-file benchmark, DuckDB was about 2x faster than Arrow for this step while writing slightly smaller parquet. Keep zstd compression, medium row groups (ROW_GROUP_SIZE 250000), and medium-sized files (ROW_GROUPS_PER_FILE 20 landed well here).
Partition only where the filter pattern justifies it. On the local layout benchmark, partitioning itr_context by INCM_YR improved year-filter queries, but the gain was small in absolute terms on local NVMe and irrelevant for one-year tables like MBS 2015. Use SET memory_limit='20GB' (or whatever leaves the OS and other processes room to breathe) and SET threads TO 8. For one-off scans and light exploration, views over parquet are fine.

That’s it. Everything else on this site is evidence that this recipe works.

title: Recommendations
text:
When to deviate

Tiny data (sub-GB, single file). Just use read_csv or read_parquet and dplyr-on-tibbles. A DuckDB connection is overkill.
Ad-hoc exploration of a parquet directory you don’t own. arrow::open_dataset() |> filter() |> collect() is faster to type than wiring up a DuckDB view. If the query runs in under a couple of seconds, arrow still wins on ergonomics.
You mostly do single-table scans and the parquet is already laid out well. DuckDB views over parquet are already very fast on this machine for T1/T2-style work. Materialised tables start paying off when the workload becomes join-heavy or you keep rerunning the same queries.
Heavy in-R post-processing on a small result. Pull the result into memory with collect() and then use data.table if you need serious speed on the already-in-memory object — for example, writing a giant loop over group-level regressions. data.table is the right tool for fast in-memory manipulation; it is the wrong tool for reading a 45 GB CSV.
You want to share the intermediate with a non-R user. Use views over parquet and distribute the parquet. Other tools (polars, pandas, duckdb-cli) read it natively.

title: Recommendations
text:
Anti-patterns to avoid

read_csv() on a file larger than RAM. Don’t. If you see read_csv near a PLIDA path, reach for DuckDB’s CSV reader or Arrow’s streaming path instead.
Calling collect() early. Once you pull a lazy query into R memory, every downstream verb runs in R and you lose DuckDB’s pushdown advantage. Keep the pipe lazy until the last step, and make sure the last step is small.
setDT(as.data.frame(arrow_table)) on big arrow objects. Same problem as read_csv — it materialises the whole thing. If you find yourself writing this, the fix is almost always to push the filter/select/aggregation upstream into arrow or DuckDB first.
Leaving DuckDB memory_limit at default. On a 32 GB machine a default DuckDB connection will happily try to use most of free RAM for a large aggregation, collide with R’s own heap, and get killed. Set the limit explicitly, somewhere around 60-70 % of physical memory.
Tiny parquet row groups. The local layout benchmark on itr_context made this painfully obvious: 10k-row groups slowed the full groupby materially in both Arrow and DuckDB. Keep them in the documented middle ground.
One enormous parquet file for a whole year of MBS claims. DuckDB and arrow both want many medium-sized parquet files so they can parallelise reads and skip row groups. max_rows_per_file = 5e6 is a reasonable default.

title: Recommendations
text:
How to think about it
The underlying principle is simple: move the computation to the data, not the data to the computation. DuckDB, arrow, and parquet are all built around letting the query engine look at the columns and row groups it actually needs without reading the rest. dplyr-on-tibbles (and data.table) is built around the opposite assumption — that the data is already in memory.
Once you internalise that, the deviations in the previous section follow naturally. dplyr is not bad; it is optimised for a different regime. PLIDA data does not live in that regime.
DuckDB winning the CSV → parquet benchmark does not imply “DuckDB for absolutely everything”. It implies something narrower and more useful: DuckDB is the best bulk converter on this machine, parquet remains the canonical intermediate, and Arrow is still a useful parquet-native query layer when the job is simple enough that you do not want to materialise a DuckDB database first.

title: Recommendations
text:
Bigger RAM, slower storage
The 32 GB MacBook with local SSD is not the only machine worth thinking about.

64 GB or 128 GB RAM with fast local SSD/NVMe. The extra RAM mostly buys headroom for larger joins, sorts, and windows before DuckDB needs to spill. It does not change the basic ranking: Parquet stays the interchange format, DuckDB tables stay the default for repeated heavy work, and Arrow stays a good low-overhead option for linear scan/filter/summarise pipelines.
64 GB or 128 GB RAM with slow network storage. More RAM does not fix network latency or low remote I/O. The DuckDB docs are quite direct here: network-attached storage is acceptable for read-only workloads, but not recommended for read-write DuckDB databases. The practical implication is to keep the canonical parquet wherever it lives, but put the working .duckdb file and DuckDB spill directory on local SSD whenever possible. If you cannot, expect views-over-parquet to become much less attractive because every repeated scan pays the network cost again.

That second point is an inference from the docs plus the shape of these benchmarks: once I/O becomes the bottleneck, local materialisation matters more, not less.

title: Recommendations
text:
Reproducing everything on this site
# 1. convert CSVs to parquet
Rscript R/01-convert-csv-to-parquet.R
Rscript R/01b-convert-supplementary.R

# 2. build the DuckDB database (~3 min)
Rscript R/02-build-duckdb.R

# 3. run the benchmarks
Rscript R/04-run-benchmarks.R

# 4. render the site
quarto render
All intermediate data (parquet + DuckDB file) lives outside the project in /Users/willmackey/offline/using-plida-data/. The project folder contains only code, a small benchmark log, and the rendered site.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
text: The previous page showed that DuckDB already crushes dplyr and arrow on a three-table PLIDA join. The obvious follow-up: can we make DuckDB itself faster by arranging the data differently on disk? The answer — after actually running the experiments — is “it depends on the query”, and the results reward thinking carefully about how zonemap pruning actually works.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
section: What DuckDB actually does under the hood
text:
What DuckDB actually does under the hood
From the DuckDB performance guide:

No B-tree indexes for analytical queries. DuckDB uses zonemaps — min and max statistics per row group (default 122,880 rows) — on every column automatically. Filters are evaluated against the zonemap first, and entire row groups are skipped if the predicate cannot possibly match.
Zonemap selectivity depends on the ordering of the column. If the data is sorted by column x, each row group covers a tight interval of x, and a filter x = k skips all but one row group. If the data is shuffled, every row group’s [min, max] overlaps every query point, and no pruning happens at all.
PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, and FOREIGN KEY constraints create ART indexes. ART indexes help highly selective point lookups but do nothing for joins, aggregations, or scans. They also slow ingest by roughly 4×. Use them for data integrity, not query performance.
Joins are hash joins. DuckDB does not do sort-merge joins. Since DuckDB 0.9 it does push min/max filters (sometimes bloom filters) from the build side of the hash join to the probe side. If the probe side is sorted by the join key, and the build side has a tight range, these pushed-down filters can prune most row groups on the probe side.
Integer joins are faster than string joins — docs cite ~1.8× on identical logical data.

The critical phrase in point (4) is “and the build side has a tight range”. I wrote this page thinking narrow-filter joins would obviously benefit from sorting MBS by the join key. The actual measurements show that hypothesis was only half right — and understanding why is the lesson of this page.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
text:
The variants
Four physical layouts of the fact table and two of the dimension table, all built in plida_variants.duckdb by R/05-storage-variants.R. Tasks are run by R/05b-run-variant-tasks.R against the same connection.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
section: Task A — point lookup for one person
text:
Task A — point lookup for one person
The simplest test, and the one sorting is built for:
SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(NUMSERV)
FROM <variant>
WHERE SYNTHETIC_AEUID = '<target>'
We pick a target AEUID with 25–40 claims (enough to be interesting but not pathological). On the unsorted table DuckDB must check every row group’s zonemap, and since the AEUID min/max on an unsorted table spans the whole key space, no pruning happens and the query scans all 371 M rows. On the sorted table all but one row group is pruned.

Sorted is roughly 50× faster than baseline and the int-key variant matches it. This is the win the DuckDB docs promise, and it is exactly what zonemap pruning is designed for: a single-value filter on a sorted column touches essentially one row group. Use this kind of layout for any fact table where you routinely ask “tell me everything about this person”.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
section: Task B — narrow-filter three-way join (birth year 1970)
text:
Task B — narrow-filter three-way join (birth year 1970)
This is where I expected a big win and did not get one. The query:
SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(NUMSERV)
FROM demo d
JOIN dhda_spine ds ON d.SPINE_ID = ds.spine_id
JOIN <mbs_variant> m ON ds.SYNTHETIC_AEUID = m.SYNTHETIC_AEUID
WHERE d.YEAR_OF_BIRTH = 1970
Demo is filtered to ~500k people first, joined through dhda_spine to produce ~500k MBS keys, and the result probes MBS. The plan seems tailor-made for sorting to help: build a hash table on the small side (500k keys), probe the giant table with a pushed-down min/max filter, done.





variant
seconds
peak_rss_gb




mbs_baseline
0.893
3.22


mbs_sorted
0.865
5.16





Essentially no difference. Why?
Because the range of AEUIDs on the build side is nearly the whole key space. People born in 1970 are uniformly distributed across the AEUID domain — birth year has nothing to do with how the PLIDA identifier was assigned. So the min/max of the build-side AEUIDs is roughly [000000000000, FFFFFFFFFFFF], and that pushed-down filter does not actually prune anything. Every MBS row group still has to be probed, and we pay the full hash-join cost regardless of sort order.
The general principle: sorting the probe side only helps when the build side covers a tight range of the join key. Filters that correlate with the join key (e.g. “give me all AEUIDs starting with 00001”) would benefit enormously. Filters that are statistically independent of the join key — birth year, gender, state of residence, most demographic filters — do not.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter

Task C — full three-way join (every person)
Same query as above but without the WHERE clause. Every person, bucketed by decade of age, summed over all of MBS 2015.

The sorted variant is slightly slower than baseline. This is expected: with no filter, no row groups can be pruned, so there is nothing for the sort ordering to buy. The sort adds a small amount of I/O and compression-dictionary overhead that does not get amortised. In other words, sorting is a pure cost in this regime.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
section: Task D — integer hash-key variant of Task B
text:
Task D — integer hash-key variant of Task B
Same narrow-filter join as Task B, but joining on an integer hash(SYNTHETIC_AEUID) column rather than the 12-character hex string.





variant

The int-key variant is roughly 1.9× faster than the string-key version of the same query — matching the docs’ ~1.8× figure almost exactly. This is a real win, but I would still treat it as an optimisation rather than the default schema for this project: a hashed surrogate key adds another column to carry through the workflow, and unlike an exact integer recode it is not formally collision-free. The safe interpretation is: if repeated string-key joins dominate your workload, benchmark a numeric surrogate key.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
section: What about the PRIMARY KEY variant?
text:
What about the PRIMARY KEY variant?
We built dhda_spine_pk with PRIMARY KEY (spine_id). The docs predict this will not help query performance, and we observe exactly that: the narrow-filter join run against dhda_spine_pk is indistinguishable from the run against plain dhda_spine. The ART index is maintained for integrity, not for joins.
The only observation worth noting is build cost: the PK fill took noticeably longer than a plain INSERT INTO of the same data, and in our first attempt it failed outright because a handful of dhda_spine rows have NULL spine_id (which the constraint rejects). If you declare constraints, declare them because you want the integrity check; expect slower writes and no faster reads.

title: Storage layout: sorting, keys, and why they matter
text:
The short version

Sort the fact table by the column you do point lookups on. That is where sorting wins, dramatically, and the win is reliable because zonemap pruning on a single-value filter is about as close to a free lunch as OLAP offers.
Sorting does not help every query. It only helps when the filter produces a contiguous range of the sorted column. Demographic filters that are uncorrelated with the join key get no benefit from sorting on that key.
Sorting full-scan workloads is mildly harmful. Do not sort tables that you only ever scan end-to-end.
If repeated string-key joins dominate, benchmark a numeric surrogate key. On this corpus the hashed-int variant was about 1.9× faster on the narrow join. That is strong enough to be worth exploring, but not strong enough to justify adding a collision-prone surrogate column to every table by default.
Do not declare PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE for performance. Declare them for correctness. Expect slower ingest and no query benefit.

The practical upshot for the PLIDA recipe in step 2 of the main pipeline is more selective than I expected when I started this page: do not sort the big fact tables by join key by default. Keep the baseline materialised table simple unless your workload is dominated by point lookups or filters that are tightly aligned with that key. If those are common, a sorted secondary copy is worth the one-off build cost. If repeated SYNTHETIC_AEUID joins dominate and the extra schema complexity is acceptable, a numeric surrogate key is the next thing to benchmark. Everything else in the main recommendations still applies.

title: Benchmarks
text:
Five backends — dplyr on in-memory tibbles, data.table, arrow datasets, DuckDB views over parquet, DuckDB materialised tables — on three tasks chosen because they come up every day in PLIDA work:

T1 filter + count. One year of ITR (~8 M rows), count rows with non-null occupation code. This is the easy one; we include it mostly to get a baseline for “small file, nothing complicated”.
T2 groupby count. All ~10 years of ITR pooled (~170 M rows), count rows per occupation group. Full scan of the biggest column-narrow table, exercise the groupby operator.
T3 join + groupby. Link demo to MBS 2015 via dhda_spine, bucket people by decade of age, sum NUMSERV. Three tables, the heaviest of them larger than RAM. This is the real test.

Every backend runs in a fresh R subprocess (R/04-run-benchmarks.R) so that memory from a prior run cannot pollute the next measurement. For the join task we do not even attempt dplyr or data.table — loading 18.7 GB of MBS parquet into a tibble or data.table immediately blows the 32 GB memory budget. We record them as intentional skips.

title: Benchmarks
text:
What the numbers mean

T1: filter + count on one year of ITR
Small-file regime. Every backend can handle this, and the raw speed difference is mostly down to fixed costs: starting a DuckDB connection and opening a parquet file is a similar order of magnitude to the actual work. The key story here is memory rather than time: dplyr and data.table both materialise the whole year’s worth of ITR (~250 MB in memory), while arrow and duckdb hold almost nothing — they stream.
This is the regime where it does not matter which backend you pick, and you might as well reach for the one with the most ergonomic API (dbplyr, in my view).


T2: groupby count on all ITR years
Medium regime. Now we are scanning ~170 M rows, about 2-3 GB of parquet. dplyr-on-tibbles is paying the full materialisation cost — it has to load every row into R memory before grouping. data.table is the same story (arrow → data.table conversion is a full copy). Arrow and DuckDB both stream the scan and only materialise the ~1000-row result.
The DuckDB table version is meaningfully faster than the DuckDB view version because DuckDB’s own storage is denser and the groupby runs against column stripes directly, without going through the parquet decoder on every query.


T3: three-way join on MBS 2015
Out-of-core regime, and the only task that actually discriminates between the backends. dplyr and data.table are not run: loading 18.7 GB of MBS parquet into a tibble on a 32 GB machine makes the machine unusable within seconds. That is not a benchmark failure — it is the reason this site exists.
In the fresh benchmark run, Arrow did not complete the task at all: the Acero join engine was killed by the OS on the 32 GB machine. That leaves two viable out-of-core options:

DuckDB/view lets DuckDB drive the parquet reader directly. It completed the join in 6.39s.
DuckDB/table is the fastest of the two, because it owns the storage. It completed the same join in 3.71s.

That is the practical dividing line in this project: Arrow is fine for scans and smaller joins; once the workload looks like a real PLIDA fact-table join, DuckDB tables are the safe default.

title: Benchmarks
text:
Peak memory









The memory plot is arguably the more important one. For the smallest task, every backend is fine. For the medium task, dplyr and data.table are already paying several GB for something that DuckDB handles in a few hundred MB. For the big task, there is no dplyr or data.table bar because there cannot be one.

title: Setup
text: Before running anything on this site, you need two things: the synthetic data, and a place to put intermediate files. This page walks through both.

title: Setup
text:
Step 1: Install fplida
fplida is an R package with a Rust backend that generates synthetic data mirroring PLIDA’s structure. It produces realistic, cross-dataset-consistent microdata for 34 administrative and survey datasets — everything from Census and tax returns to MBS claims and STP payroll.
# Requires R 4.5+ and Rust 1.68+ (via rustup)
export PATH="$HOME/.cargo/bin:$PATH"

# Clone and install
git clone https://github.com/wfmackey/fplida.git
R CMD INSTALL fplida/

title: Setup
text:
Step 2: Generate the data
The size you generate depends on what you want to test. The case studies on this site use 30 million people (~750 GB of CSV), but you can start much smaller:

The total disk size understates the challenge — it’s the dataset-level row counts that matter. PLIDA datasets are not one-row-per-person; MBS has ~12 claims per person per year, STP has ~52 payroll records per employed person per year, and the ITR has multiple sub-tables per person per year. Even at 1 million people, joins across these tables involve tens of millions of rows:
